source_metadata.title_raw: Christian M. Carmona - I-19-005 - January 28, 2019 - Fairfield

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 28, 2019

Christian M. Carmona, Risk Manager
Liability/Litigation/Insurance
Finance Department
City of Fairfield
1000 Webster Street
Fairfield, CA 94533

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-19-005

Dear Mr. Carmona:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your inquiry as a request for formal assistance.²

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

As an employee of the City, may you obtain outside employment consulting with George Hills during your time off when George Hills has a contract for services with the City?

_____________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-19-005
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

The Act does not prohibit you from consulting with George Hills so long as you do not
make, participate in making or attempt to use your official position to influence a decision before
the City that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on George Hills. However,
we caution that your agency may impose restrictions on incompatible activities outside of the Act
which may restrict your ability to accept this position. You should consult your agency's counsel
regarding your agency's restrictions on incompatible activities.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are the recently hired Liability Risk Manager and Liability Claims Administrator for
the City of Fairfield. Prior to taking your current position with the City you were employed with
George Hills from 2015-2018. As a George Hills employee, you handled the City of Fairfield
liability claims. Now that the City has hired you, you will be assuming the liability claims
administration duties as part of your position with the City and George Hills will no longer be
providing this service to the City. However, George Hills is going to continue to host and enter the
City of Fairfield claims data for a flat contracted rate, which is significantly less than what they
were paying for a full service third party claims administrator. The City is allowing you to consult
on your time off and you would like to consult with George Hills for a supervisory role and training
of new adjusters on how to handle claims. You would never be in a position to make decisions in
your capacity as a City employee to benefit George Hills financially. You will also not be making
any governmental decisions regarding George Hills' contract with the City for hosting claims data.
You are a designated employee in the City's conflict of interest code.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or
more of the official's financial interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) Section 87103 sets forth the
official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act.

An official has a financial interest in a source of income aggregating $500 or more in value
promised to or received by the official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision at issue
is made. (Section 87103(c).) An official also has a financial interest in any business entity in which
the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee or employee or holds any position of management.
(Regulation 18700(c)(6)(D).) You have a source of income interest in George Hills your former
employer assuming you received compensation from George Hills of $500 or more in the past 12
months and will receive such income should you obtain future employment with George Hills. As
an employee of George Hills, you will also have a financial interest in the company as a business
entity.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701 sets forth the Act's standards for determining whether a decision's
financial effect on an official's financial interest is reasonably foreseeable. A decision's effect on an
official's financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party

File No. I-19-005
Page No. 3

in, or the subject of, the decision. Subdivision (b) of the same regulation provides the foreseeability
standard applicable to an official's interest which is not explicitly involved in the decisions at issue:

"In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably
foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary
circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not
reasonably foreseeable."

Regulation 18702.3 provides that Regulation 18702.1 contains the applicable materiality
standards for sources of income that are business entities. Regulation 18702.1 also provides the
applicable materiality standards for your financial interest in George Hills as a business entity.

You have not provided any facts regarding decisions you will be making in your capacity as
a City employee with regard to George Hills, though stated that you will not be involved in any
governmental decisions that would involve or affect George Hills financially. The Act does not
prohibit you from taking outside employment with George Hills. The Act only prohibits you from
making, participating in making, or attempting to influence decisions by the City with a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on your financial interest in George Hill. Should a decision
come before you in your capacity as a City employee that would involve or affect George Hills, you
may wish to consult your agency's attorney or seek further advice.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl
